threats.title: Global Weight Sum Corruption via Nominee Removal
threats.hypothesis_description: The `removeNominee` function in `VoteWeighting` updates the bias of the global weight sum (`pointsSum[nextTime].bias`) to reflect the removal of a nominee, but fails to subtract the removed nominee's slope from `pointsSum[nextTime].slope`. This causes the global weight sum to decay faster than the actual sum of active weights, eventually corrupting relative weight calculations for all active nominees and disrupting the entire emission distribution system.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a critical logic error in the core governance contract. If an admin removes a deprecated staking target, it inadvertently corrupts the global slope. We need to validate the exact impact on `nomineeRelativeWeight` calculations over subsequent epochs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `pointsSum[nextTime].bias = newSum;` is executed, but `pointsSum[nextTime].slope` is not updated to remove the slope of the removed nominee.

preconditions: Admin calls `removeNominee` on an active nominee with a non-zero slope.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Incomplete state update during nominee removal.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin to remove a nominee, which is a normal operational procedure for deprecated targets.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent corruption starting from the next epoch after removal.

operational_questions: How quickly does the corrupted slope cause `pointsSum` to reach zero or underflow?
Does this completely halt all staking incentive distributions?
validation_requirements: Simulate a nominee removal with active votes and advance time to observe `pointsSum` decay.
Check if `nomineeRelativeWeight` reverts due to division by zero or returns incorrect proportions.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for admin to legitimately remove a deprecated nominee.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Observe `pointsSum` decaying at an accelerated rate due to the orphaned slope.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: As `pointsSum` drops artificially low, the relative weights of remaining nominees become distorted.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Eventually, `pointsSum` may hit zero prematurely, halting all emissions.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None; triggered by normal admin action.

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: None directly, but breaks core protocol functionality.
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete failure of the staking incentive distribution system.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires a contract upgrade or complex state migration to fix the corrupted `pointsSum` history.

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time a nominee is removed.

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: `pointsSum` decaying faster than the sum of individual nominee slopes.

threats.title: L1 Staking Incentive Batch DoS via Disabled Target
threats.hypothesis_description: In `EthereumDepositProcessor._deposit`, if any single staking target in a batch returns a zero emissions limit (e.g., if it was disabled by its deployer), the entire transaction reverts with `TargetEmissionsZero`. An attacker can deploy a staking instance, get it included in a batch, and then disable it to DoS the entire L1 incentive distribution for that batch.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike L2 dispensers which gracefully withhold amounts for invalid targets and continue processing the batch, the L1 processor uses a hard revert. This creates a griefing vector where one malicious or misconfigured target blocks rewards for all other targets in the same batch.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `if (limitAmount == 0) { revert TargetEmissionsZero(target); }` inside a loop over all targets.

preconditions: A batch of L1 staking incentives is processed via `sendMessageBatch`.
At least one target in the batch returns a 0 emissions limit (e.g., disabled via `StakingFactory.setInstanceStatus`).

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Hard revert in a batch processing loop instead of graceful failure/withholding.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None; any deployer can disable their own instance.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a batch is processed containing the disabled target.

operational_questions: Can the batch be split or processed individually to bypass the DoS?
Is there any way for the protocol admin to force-enable the malicious target?
validation_requirements: Deploy a target, vote for it, disable it, and attempt to process a batch containing it alongside valid targets.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker deploys a staking instance via `StakingFactory`.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker (or others) votes for the instance to ensure it receives some allocation.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Right before the epoch ends or the batch is processed, attacker calls `setInstanceStatus(instance, false)`.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The `sendMessageBatch` transaction reverts, blocking all other L1 targets from receiving their incentives.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires enough veOLAS to get the malicious target included in the batch.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete halt of L1 staking incentive distributions for affected batches.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires governance to remove the malicious nominee from `VoteWeighting`.

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Minimal amount to register a vote.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Deployer disabling a staking instance that is currently receiving votes.

threats.title: Queued Redemptions Bypass Emissions Limit Verification
threats.hypothesis_description: When a staking target is queued due to insufficient OLAS balance in `DefaultTargetDispenserL2`, the `redeem` function allows claiming the queued amount without re-verifying the target's validity or emissions limit. If a target is disabled or its limit is reduced after queuing, it can still claim the full queued amount.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates a time-of-check to time-of-use (TOCTOU) vulnerability. The emissions limit is checked when the message is received, but the actual transfer happens later during `redeem`. If the target's status changes in between, the protocol distributes tokens to an invalid target.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `redeem` checks `stakingQueueingNonces[queueHash]` but does not call `verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount` before transferring OLAS.

preconditions: L2 dispenser has insufficient OLAS balance when a bridge message arrives, causing the deposit to be queued.
The staking target's emissions limit is reduced or the target is disabled after queuing but before redemption.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing re-verification of external state during a delayed execution path.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: The time between a deposit being queued and redeemed.

operational_questions: How often do L2 dispensers experience insufficient balance causing queuing?
Can an attacker intentionally drain the L2 dispenser balance to force queuing?
validation_requirements: Queue a valid deposit, disable the target via `StakingFactory`, fund the dispenser, and successfully call `redeem`.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker ensures L2 dispenser has low balance (e.g., by claiming other valid rewards).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Bridge message arrives for attacker's valid target, queuing the deposit.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker disables their target or lowers its emissions limit.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker funds the dispenser and calls `redeem`, receiving OLAS that should have been withheld.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires manipulating L2 dispenser balance to force queuing.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Distribution of OLAS to invalid or over-limit staking targets.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - funds are lost but system continues.

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Understanding of cross-chain timing and queue mechanisms.
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: During periods of low L2 dispenser balance.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Target status changes while it has pending queued redemptions.

threats.title: Factory Owner Lacks Authority to Disable Malicious Staking Instances
threats.hypothesis_description: `StakingFactory.setInstanceStatus` only allows the original `deployer` to enable or disable a staking instance. If a deployer turns malicious or their key is compromised, the factory owner has no administrative capability to disable the instance at the factory level.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a significant operational gap. While governance can remove the nominee from `VoteWeighting`, any pending bridge messages or queued redemptions for the malicious instance cannot be stopped by the factory owner, leading to unavoidable fund loss during the governance delay.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `if (msg.sender != deployer) { revert OwnerOnly(msg.sender, deployer); }` - no override for `owner`.

preconditions: A staking instance is deployed and receiving emissions.
The instance becomes malicious or the deployer's key is compromised.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Strict access control tying instance lifecycle exclusively to the permissionless deployer.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Protocol admins are locked out of emergency response.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Until governance successfully removes the nominee and all pending messages clear.

operational_questions: Is there any other mechanism to block the instance (e.g., via `StakingVerifier`)?
Can the verifier be updated to reject the specific instance?
validation_requirements: Verify that the factory owner cannot call `setInstanceStatus`.
Check if updating the verifier is a viable emergency response.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker deploys a valid staking instance and gains votes.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker upgrades the implementation (if possible) or exploits a flaw in the instance.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Protocol admins attempt to disable the instance via `StakingFactory` but revert.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker continues extracting funds while governance vote is pending.

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Inability to perform emergency incident response.
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Continued loss of funds to a known malicious instance.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires governance vote to remove nominee or update verifier.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed attempts by protocol admins to call `setInstanceStatus`.

threats.title: Public Staking Calculation Function Modifies VoteWeighting State
threats.hypothesis_description: `Dispenser.calculateStakingIncentives` is a public function that calls `IVoteWeighting(voteWeighting).checkpointNominee(stakingTarget, chainId)`, which modifies state. Anyone can call this function to force checkpoints on nominees, potentially manipulating the exact timing of weight updates or causing gas griefing.
threats.investigation_rationale: The documentation comments state: 'Call this function via staticcall in order not to write in the nominee checkpoint map.' However, there is no enforcement of this. An attacker can call it directly as a transaction to alter the state of `VoteWeighting` at specific times, which may affect relative weight calculations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `IVoteWeighting(voteWeighting).checkpointNominee(stakingTarget, chainId);` inside a `public` function without access control.

preconditions: Attacker calls `calculateStakingIncentives` as a state-changing transaction.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Public function intended for view/staticcall actually modifies critical governance state.

operational_questions: Does forcing a nominee checkpoint at a specific block alter its relative weight advantageously?
Can this be used to grief the protocol by advancing checkpoints prematurely?
validation_requirements: Analyze the impact of calling `checkpointNominee` mid-week vs end-of-week on `nomineeRelativeWeight`.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls `calculateStakingIncentives` to force a checkpoint on a specific nominee at an optimal time.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: This locks in a specific weight or slope before other users can react.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker claims incentives based on the manipulated state.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: EOAs calling `calculateStakingIncentives` directly instead of via `claimStakingIncentives`.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpectedly frequent checkpoints for specific nominees.

threats.title: Donation Amount Truncation Causes Reward Discrepancy
threats.hypothesis_description: In `Tokenomics._trackServiceDonations`, the donation amount is divided by `numServiceUnits` and cast to `uint96`. The remainder of the division is lost from the unit's pending relative reward but is still added to the `totalDonationsETH`. This leads to a discrepancy where ETH is held by the treasury but never distributed to component/agent owners.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the amount lost per transaction is small (up to `numServiceUnits - 1` wei), it systematically favors services with fewer units and causes ETH to become permanently locked in the Treasury without an accounting record in `Tokenomics`.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `uint96 amount = uint96(amounts[i] / numServiceUnits);` inside a loop.

preconditions: A donation is made to a service where `amounts[i]` is not perfectly divisible by `numServiceUnits`.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Integer division before accumulation causes precision loss.

operational_questions: How much ETH accumulates as dust in the Treasury over time?
Is there a mechanism to recover this unallocated ETH?
validation_requirements: Calculate the maximum possible dust accumulation per epoch based on expected transaction volume.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Truncation occurs on every donation, leaving wei in the Treasury.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Over millions of transactions, significant ETH may become unclaimable.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Slow leakage of value into an unrecoverable state.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires Treasury upgrade to sweep dust.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Treasury ETH balance exceeding the sum of all pending and claimable rewards in Tokenomics.

threats.title: Unrevoked Votes on Removed Nominees Distort Global Weight Sum
threats.hypothesis_description: When a nominee is removed via `VoteWeighting.removeNominee`, users' existing votes (slopes) for that nominee are not automatically canceled. If users do not manually call `revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower`, their scheduled slope changes will still apply to `changesSum` at their lock expiration times, artificially reducing the total weight sum and inflating the relative weights of remaining nominees.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol relies on users voluntarily spending gas to clean up state for a removed nominee. If users are inactive or apathetic, the global accounting (`changesSum`) becomes permanently corrupted, leading to incorrect emission distributions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower` must be called manually by each user to decrement `changesSum[oldEnd]`.

preconditions: Admin removes a nominee that has active votes.
Voters fail to call `revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower` before their veOLAS lock expires.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State cleanup relies on voluntary user action with no economic incentive.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between nominee removal and voters' lock expiration.

operational_questions: What happens to `pointsSum` when a scheduled `changesSum` decrement occurs for a slope that was already zeroed out?
Does this cause `pointsSum.slope` to become negative or underflow?
validation_requirements: Simulate a scenario where a heavily voted nominee is removed and users do not revoke votes, then advance time past lock expirations.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker votes heavily for a nominee they know will be removed.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker intentionally does not revoke their voting power.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: When the attacker's lock expires, `changesSum` is decremented, corrupting the global slope and distorting emissions for other targets.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Incorrect distribution of staking incentives across all chains.

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: High amount of veOLAS to make a noticeable impact.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Users with expired locks who voted for removed nominees but haven't called revoke.

threats.title: Permanent Tokenomics Freeze via Prolonged Checkpoint Delay
threats.hypothesis_description: If `Tokenomics.checkpoint()` is not called for a duration exceeding `MAX_EPOCH_LENGTH` (~364 days), it will permanently return `false`. Because `checkpoint()` is required to advance the epoch, the tokenomics system becomes permanently frozen, halting all reward distributions, bond programs, and inflation minting.
threats.investigation_rationale: While highly unlikely under normal operations, this represents a strict state transition failure mode. A prolonged network outage, governance failure, or extreme gas griefing could trigger this, resulting in a completely bricked protocol.
preconditions: No user or bot calls `checkpoint()` for `MAX_EPOCH_LENGTH` seconds.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Hard upper bound on time between state transitions with no recovery mechanism.

operational_questions: Is there any admin override to force an epoch transition if this limit is breached?
Can the proxy implementation be upgraded to remove this check if it happens?
validation_requirements: Verify that `checkpoint` permanently fails after the time limit.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Protocol experiences extreme inactivity or attacker continuously griefs `checkpoint` transactions.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Time since last epoch end exceeds `MAX_EPOCH_LENGTH`.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Extremely high (requires preventing all users from calling a public function for a year).

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete and permanent halt of the tokenomics system.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires upgrading the Tokenomics implementation.
